coredata.dc:title: Carbonized polymer dots functionalized graphene to regulate lithium homogenous deposition and SEI formation dynamics
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Guo-Duo
Li, Yan-Fei
Ding, Lei
Wang, Zhuo
Zhou, Su-Min
Wu, Xing-Long
Yang, Jin
Sun, Hai-Zhu
coredata.dc:description: Flexible lithium metal anode with ultrahigh theoretical specific capacity suffers from uncontrollable growth of lithium dendrite and severe volume changes during Li plating/stripping process. To address these issues, a novel kind of flexible graphene oxide (GO) film anchored with carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) (CPD@GO) composite substrate was fabricated via a one-step microwave approach in which CPDs successfully regulate the dispersion and interlayer spacing of GO. Nanoscale CPDs act as lithiophilic nucleation sites to promote Li uniform deposition between the interlayers rather than on the surface of GO. The abundant surface functional groups of CPDs result in the generation of more stable components for solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) with high ionic conductivity of LiF and Li3N through accelerating the degradation dynamics of lithium salt. As a result, the obtained lithium anode presents small fluctuant profiles with lower voltage hysteresis (10.5 mV after 2600 h of operation at 1.0 mA cm−2). The coin and pouch full cells constructed with LiFePO4 remain decent cycling performance. This work paves a new sight for the application of carbon materials in energy storage devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized polymer dots
Lithiophilic functional groups
Lithium metal anode
In-situ growth
Solid electrolyte interphase

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structure and composition of self-supporting CPD@GO
Lithium deposition behavior of self-supporting CPD@GO
SEI composition regulation of self-supporting CPD@GO

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22275030 and 22035001 ), Jilin Provincial Education Department and Science and Technology Achievements Transformation Fund Project of Northeast Normal University ( CGZH202203 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of CPDs growth on GO in-situ via one step microwave. (b, c) TEM images of CPD@GO-3 (Inset: HRTEM image of CPD@GO-3). (d) XRD pattern of CPD@GO-1, CPD@GO-3, CPD@GO-6 and GO. High-resolution XPS of (e) C 1 s for GO and CPD@GO-3. (f) N 1 s of CPD@GO-3. (g) Raman spectrum of GO and CPD@GO-3.

(a) The voltage-capacity curves of CPD@RGO-3 and RGO electrode during Li nucleation at 1 mA cm−2. (b) Nucleation overpotential of tow electrodes at different current densities. (c) CE of Li deposition on different electrodes at 1 mA cm−2 with the fixed capacity of 0.5 mAh cm−2. (d, e) Voltage-time profiles of symmetric Li|Li@CPD@RGO-3 and Li|Li@RGO cells. (f, g) Nyquist plots of electrochemical impedance test for CPD@RGO-3, RGO after 10, 50 and 80 cycles.

Top view and Cross-sectional view SEM images of CPD@RGO-3 (a-d) and RGO (e-h) loaded with Li deposition of 1 mAh cm−2 and 5 mAh cm−2. Schematic illustration of the lithium plating behaviors on the (i) CPD@RGO-3 and (j) RGO.
(a) The full survey XPS spectrum of Li@CPD@RGO-3 and Li@RGO electrodes after 10 cycles at 1 mA cm−2. High resolution XPS of (b) C 1 s, (c) F 1 s, (d) Li 1 s, and (e) N 1 s.

(a) Rate performance of Li||LFP and Li@CPD@RGO-3||LFP cells. (b) cycling performances of bare Li||LFP and Li@CPD@RGO-3||LFP cells at 0.5C. Charge/discharge curves of particular cycles for (c) Li@CPD@RGO-3||LFP and (d) Li||LFP at 0.5C. (e) The cycling performances of Li@CPD@RGO-3||LFP pouch cell and light testing of Li@CPD@RGO-3||LFP pouch cells in flat and bending states.

head.ce:title: Carbonized polymer dots functionalized graphene to regulate lithium homogenous deposition and SEI formation dynamics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, National & Local United Engineering Laboratory for Power Batteries, Northeast Normal University, No. 5268 Renmin Street, Changchun 130024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, National & Local United Engineering Laboratory for Power Batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, National & Local United Engineering Laboratory for Power Batteries, Northeast Normal University, No. 5268 Renmin Street, Changchun 130024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Polyoxometalate and Reticular Material Chemistry of Ministry of Education, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Polyoxometalate and Reticular Material Chemistry of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Polyoxometalate and Reticular Material Chemistry of Ministry of Education, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Flexible lithium metal anode with ultrahigh theoretical specific capacity suffers from uncontrollable growth of lithium dendrite and severe volume changes during Li plating/stripping process. To address these issues, a novel kind of flexible graphene oxide (GO) film anchored with carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) (CPD@GO) composite substrate was fabricated via a one-step microwave approach in which CPDs successfully regulate the dispersion and interlayer spacing of GO. Nanoscale CPDs act as lithiophilic nucleation sites to promote Li uniform deposition between the interlayers rather than on the surface of GO. The abundant surface functional groups of CPDs result in the generation of more stable components for solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) with high ionic conductivity of LiF and Li3N through accelerating the degradation dynamics of lithium salt. As a result, the obtained lithium anode presents small fluctuant profiles with lower voltage hysteresis (10.5 mV after 2600 h of operation at 1.0 mA cm−2). The coin and pouch full cells constructed with LiFePO4 remain decent cycling performance. This work paves a new sight for the application of carbon materials in energy storage devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium (Li) metal with ultrahigh theoretical specific capacity (3860 mAh g−1) and the lowest redox potential (-3.04 V vs. standard hydrogen electrode) is considered as one of the most promising candidates for the rechargeable high-energy–density batteries [1–3]. However, the practical applications of Li metal are hindered by growth of Li dendrites and volume changes during the repeated cycling, resulting in the low Coulombic efficiency (CE), poor cycle life and safety hazards [4–10].

To solve the above-mentioned issues, carbon-based materials have been used to construct the three-dimensional (3D) host for Li metal due to their flexibility and lightweight [11–15]. Among the carbon-based materials, graphene oxide (GO)-based materials with desirable oxygen functional groups and large specific surfaces have been used as promising hosts for Li deposition [16–18]. To improve the electrical conductivity of GO-based materials, thermal reduction treatment is normally inevitably performed, which results in the flexibility loss and functional group reduction. Therefore, high Li nucleation overpotential is usually obtained in GO due to its poor lithiophilicity and insufficient lithiophilic sites. In addition, the issue of graphene agglomeration can not be solved, which is detrimental to achieve the uniform Li deposition both in the interlayer and on their surface owing to the inadequate exposure of nucleation sites. Furthermore, empirical observations show that Li hardly deposits into the interlayer of GO due to the lack of lithiophililc sites and strong interlayer forces [19,20]. To solve the lithiophobic problem, heteroatomic doping and noble metal particle introduction have been developed [20–23]. For instance, Zhang et al. reported the S-doped graphene, improving the Li adsorption ability to reduce the uneven Li deposition [22]. However, the realization of heteroatoms doping strategy is intrinsically based on destructing the intact structure of graphene materials, resulting in the decreased electron transport dynamics [24–26]. In addition, the introduction of precious metal particles increases the cost.

Generally, the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) is formed by the side reaction of electrolyte on the electrode surface. The ideal SEI can effectively inhibit the further consumption of electrolyte, and stabilize the electrode. The stable SEI has also been proved to be important for suppressing the formation of the dendritic Li [6,27–29]. The recent reports have proven the SEI layer containing LiF and Li3N provides excellent protection for Li anode. Lou and co-workers [30] designed the self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) with the long-range-ordered polar carboxyl group to promote the formation of SEI enriched LiF and suppress dendrite Li growth. Thus, it is desirable to regulate the generation of SEI while inducing uniform lithium deposition. However, the regulation of the SEI composition and structure is still a great challenge.

Herein, based on the functional groups’ reaction between the carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) and GO, a flexible layered GO film anchored with CPDs (CPD@GO) was in situ prepared as stable Li metal host via a one-step microwave method. Carbon dots (CDs), especially CPDs [31,32], with small size less than 10 nm, a large number of functional groups, environmentally friendly and low cost, have attracted much attention in energy storage field [33,34]. The developed method ensures to generate lots of lithiophilic functional groups, which reduce the barrier of Li nucleation. The introduction of CPDs regulate the agglomeration and interlayer spacing of GO, enhancing Li deposition kinetics between layers. The CPDs containing lithiophilic functional groups of –COOH and –NH2 also guide the composition of the stable SEI by accelerating the degradation dynamics of lithium salt. As a result, the designed hybrid substrate displays interfacial stability and maintain a decent CE of 96.0 % for 130 cycles at 1 mA cm−2 during Li plating/stripping. The voltage hysteresis of symmetric cell is only 10.5 mV after 2600 h at 1 mA cm−2 with a capacity of 1 mAh cm−2.

The Li metal was purchased from DoDoChem. The electrolyte composition, 1,3-dioxolane (DOL) and 1,2-dimethoxyethane (DME) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and the lithium bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide (LiTFSI) was produced by Macklin, prepared in an Ar filled glove-box. Concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %), and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were obtained from Sinopharm Group Co., Ltd. Graphite powder, lithium nitrate (LiNO3, 99.98 %) was obtained from Aldrich, and LiFePO4 (LFP) was obtained from Guangdong Canrd New Energy Technology Co., Ltd. The reagents were not further purified.

Synthesis of GO used modified Hummer synthesis method. The experimental steps were as follows: 2 g graphite powder, 27 mL phosphoric acid and 240 mL concentrated sulfuric acid (40 mL each time, six times) were successively added to a 500 mL round-bottom flask, and mixed about 30 min. 12 g KMnO4 was transferred to the above solution in a small number of each time under strong stirring at room temperature (pay attention to the reaction temperature). After the solution cooling to room temperature, it was placed in an oil-bath and slowly heated to 55 °C. The peeling reaction runs 12 h at constant temperature. After the reaction finished, the solution was cooled to room temperature and transferred to pre-frozen ice containing 255 mL water and 15 mL H2O2. The resulting orange-yellow solution was continuously stirred and cooled to room temperature. The solution was washed with HCl solution (HCl: H2O volume ratio of 1:2) and deionized water by centrifugation at different speeds for several times until the suspension pH was the same as water, and the viscous brown GO liquid is obtained, which is freeze-dried for later use.

50 mg GO powder was added into the 50 mL deionized water to form a homogeneous solution with the assistance of ultrasound and agitation. Then, 30 mmol citric acid (CA) and 60 mmol ethylenediamine (EDA) were incorporated into the solution. After fully mixed, the uniform solution was heated in the microwave oven (700 W) for five minutes. After cooling to the room temperature, the product was diluted with water to 50 mL solution and divided evenly into two parts for vacuum filtration using filtration membrane (0.22 μm). The flexible CPD@GO paper was gain after drying at room temperature. And we prepared the molar mass of CA: EDA for 10 mmol: 20 mmol, 30 mmol: 60 mmol and 60 mmol: 120 mmol to obtain the samples named CPD@GO-1, CPD@GO-3, CPD@GO-6. GO paper was also prepared using the above methods without microwave heating.

Morphologic and elemental characteristics of the CPD@GO paper was characterized by field-emission SEM (HITACHI SU-8010) and TEM (JEOL 2100F). XRD data were obtained using Smartlab with Cu Kα radiation. XPS measurement (Thermo Fisher Scientific) was performed to investigate the chemical structure and element content. Surface characteristic functional groups were characterized by infrared tests using Agilent Technologies Cray 630 FTIR. Raman spectroscopy was performed using a Horiba Lab RAM HR Evolution. A vacuum transfer container was used to transfer the sample into the vacuum transfer chamber of the XPS system to avoid air exposure. For SEM characterizations, after preparing the sample in the glove box, it was quickly transferred to the vacuum system of SEM with the protection of a ziplock bag.

Coin cells (CR2032) were assembled in glove box filled argon (<1 ppm of water and oxygen). CPD@GO-3 paper was cut 12 mm circle electrode and directly used as anode, and the lithium metal and Celgard 2400 were used as the counter electrode and separator, respectively. 1 M lithium bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide (LiTFSI) dissolved in 1,3-dioxolane/1,2-dimethoxyethane (DOL/DME, volume ratio of 1:1) with 1 wt% LiNO3 was selected as the electrolyte. The content of electrolyte is 100 μL in each cell. To test the XRD pattern of CPD@GO, GO electrode after 3 cycles at the range of 0.01–1 V in half cells, the electrode was washed by small amount of DOL for remove the salt on the surface of them. The CPD@RGO-3 was predeposited 5 mAh cm−2 of Li to use as the anode for assembling the symmetric and full cells, respectively. For the preparation of the LiFePO4 (LFP) cathode, weighed LFP powder, acetylene black and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) binder (7:2:1, m/m/m) were all dispersed in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone solvent. The mixed slurry was cast on Al foil and then dried in an oven at 60 °C overnight. The mass weight of LFP was controlled about 2 mg cm−2. 1 M LiPF6 in EC/DEC (1:1 v/v) was used as electrolyte and its amount in each cell was controlled at 100 μL. The voltage window of full cells was controlled between 2.5 and 4.0 V at various current densities. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed at 5 mV AC amplitude with a frequency range from 0.1 MHz to 0.01 Hz.

The pouch full cells were assembled by using Al-plastic file, Ni external tabs, glass fiber separator, electrolyte, Li@CPD@RGO-3 (pre-deposited lithium of 5 mAh cm−2 on the composite electrode cut into 2.5 × 3 cm), LFP cathode cast onto the Al foil (2.5 × 3 cm, about 2 mg cm−2). The pouch cells were encapsulated by heat sealing peak-beater in Ar-filled glove-box.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and composition of self-supporting CPD@GO
ce:section.ce:para: From Fig. 1 a, the condensation reaction between citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) firstly occurs on the surface of GO flake to produce polymer chains via fast one-step microwave method. After the crosslinked and carbonized procedures, the polymer chains transform into carbon nucleus in-situ on GO flake, accompanying generation of abundant O/N-containing functional groups [32,35]. The color of GO substrate completely changes from yellow to black, indicating the surface of GO flakes is evenly covered by CPDs (depending on the concentration of the reactants, the substrates are named as CPD@GO-1, CPD@GO-3, CPD@GO-6) (Fig. S1a, c and Fig. S2a, d).

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey also confirms the presence of C, N, and O ( Fig. S7
a). N element is determined to be 7.33 a.t. % for CPD@GO-3 by XPS result. ( Table S1 ). In the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of GO (Fig. 1e), four peaks located at 284.6, 285.6, 286.4, and 288.2 eV are assigned to CC (sp2), CC (sp3), CO and CO, respectively. While in the CPD@GO, except for CC (284.6), CO (286.4) and CO (288.2), the CN (285.3 eV) and O-CO (289.0 eV) peaks are also observed [36,37]. In the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum, the two fitted peaks at 531.5 and 532.4 eV are detected in both samples ( Fig. S7
b), which are attributed to CO/OH and CO [35,38]. In the N 1 s spectrum of CPD@GO (Fig. 1f), three peaks located at 398.4, 399.6 and 401.0 eV belong to pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, and graphitic-N, respectively. Pyrrolic-N and pyridinic-N (40.45 % and 6.09 % in CN bonds, Table S2 ) with extra electrons increase the affinity with Li+, which induce homogeneous deposition of Li without the Li dendrites [39,40]. Likewise, the various O/N functional groups are also detected on the surface of CPD@GO-3 from Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) result ( Fig. S7
c). The above results prove that the composite materials obtained by one-step microwave are able to retain more functional groups compared to thermal reduction method. The lithiophilic functional groups raise the wettability of GO films with Li and homogenize the Li+ flux [41,42].

For the Raman spectra of CPD@GO and GO (Fig. 1g), the peaks at 1350 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1 display in both materials, which correspond to D (disorders and defects) and G bands (graphitic carbon) [43]. The intensity of I D/I G increases from 0.83 (GO) to 1.06 (CPD@GO-3), demonstrating that CPD@GO-3 has more defects [21,23]. The defects from CPDs as nucleation sites also contribute to homogeneous Li deposition.

The voltage profile of CPD@RGO-3 and RGO substrates is shown in Fig. 2d, e. Two substrates were pre-electrodeposited with 5 mAh cm-2Li at 0.5 mA cm−2. The Li@CPD@RGO-3 shows decent cycling stability at 1.0 mA cm−2 with capacity of 1.0 mAh cm−2 (Fig. 2d). The voltage hysteresis is 38.1 mV initially and stabilizes at ∼ 10.5 mV for 2600 h. In contrast, a sharp increase in voltage is detected for Li@RGO, which is attributed to the unstable SEI and dendritic Li growth. As current density increases to 2 mA cm−2, the Li||Li@CPD@RGO-3 cell operates without apparent change after 1100 h with a voltage hysteresis of 14.0 mV (Fig. 2e). Nevertheless, the Li||Li@RGO cell display inferior stability and dramatic value of voltage polarization. Compared with similar literatures in recent years ( Table S6 ), symmetric cells assembled with CPD@RGO-3 anode demonstrate better electrochemical performance After operating 80 cycles, the morphology of Li is shown in Fig. S13 . The homogeneous and flat Li is tightly tiled on the surface of CPD@RGO-3, while the obvious noodle-like Li appears on RGO. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the two cells in the specific cycles was tested at 1.0 mA cm−2 (Fig. 2f, g). The contact impedance of CPD@RGO-3 is lower than RGO at different cycles, and the interfacial resistance gradually decreases with the cycle proceeding, indicating the formation of a stable SEI with a lower resistance and excellent stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lithium deposition behavior of self-supporting CPD@GO
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the availability of CPDs during Li plating, the Li deposition behavior evolution images with different loading capacity were observed at 0.5 mA cm−2. From the images of Fig. 3 a, the morphology of Li plated on CPD@RGO-3 shows even deposition with 1 mAh cm−2 capacity, and the Li can be observed between layers from the cross-side view (Fig. 3b). While heterogeneous Li appears disorderly on the RGO surface and it is not found in the interlayer (Fig. 3e, f). As the amount of Li deposited increases to 5 mAh cm−2, the Li morphology plating on CPD@RGO-3 remains homogeneous and no pulverized Li appears in preferential orientation of Li (Fig. 3c, d). Nevertheless, obvious noodle-like, loose and uneven Li is scattered on the RGO surface (Fig. 3g, h). Based on the above deposition images, schematic illustration of the Li plating behaviors on the CPD@RGO-3 and RGO electrodes is illustrated in Fig. 3i-j. The Li deposition remains more stable on CPD@RGO-3 substrates. On the one hand, the presence of nanoscale CPDs with lithiophilic functional groups is able to serve as nucleation sites to significantly modulate the electric field, inducing uniform Li nucleation and growth of Li between the graphene layers and on the surface. On the other hand, the carbon nucleus with high crystallinity inside the CPD facilitates the electron transport at the electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEI composition regulation of self-supporting CPD@GO
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the role of functional groups in stabilizing the electrolyte/electrode interface, XPS tests of RGO and CPD@RGO-3 electrodes after 10 cycles at 1 mA cm−2 were employed to investigate the elemental components of the SEI. The Li, C, N, O, and F are detected on the surface of RGO and CPD@RGO-3 (Fig. 4 a). In the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4b), the peaks at 284.65, 286.6, 288.6 and 289.9 eV corresponded to C–C, COR, COOR and
CO
3
2-
, respectively. Generally, the
CO
3
2-
is electrolyte solvent decomposition products. The higher content of
CO
3
2-
indicates a higher degree of solvents degradation, which is not conducive to the stability of the battery [42,50]. Comparison of the fitted peak areas at 289.9 eV demonstrates that there is less
CO
3
2-
on CPD@RGO-3 surface. Therefore, the lower degree of degradation of electrolyte solvents is shown in CPD@RGO-3 battery construction.

The full cells are assembled with LiFePO4 (LFP) cathodes to explore the practical application of Li@CPD@RGO-3 anodes. The rate performance is shown in Fig. 5 a, the Li@CPD@RGO-3||LFP cell displays reversible discharge specific capacitys (Cs) of 154.6, 151.8, 145.2, 133.5, 111.6 and 87.3 mAh g−1 at 0.2C, 0.3C, 0.5C, 1C, 2C and 3C, respectively. When the current goes back to 0.2C, the discharge capacity still remains 154.2 mAh g−1. However, the Li||LFP cells clearly exhibit inferior rate performance and lower capacity under the same conditions. This result is attributed to a more stable SEI on the Li@CPD@RGO-3 composite anodes, which reduces the loss of capacity. In addition, Li@CPD@RGO-3||LFP cells display more decent cycling stability than Li||LFP cells. Li@CPD@RGO-3||LFP cells keep a reversible Cs of 146.0 mAh g−1 with a CE of 99.4 % for 110th cycle at 0.5C (Fig. 5b), while the CE of Li||LFP cells begin dropping at ∼ 80th cycle, which is caused by the cell over-charge. From the charge/discharge curves (Fig. 5c, d), the Li@CPD@RGO-3||LFP cells have good repeatability, small polarization voltage and capacity fading, indicating decent kinetic stability of Li@CPD@RGO-3||LFP cells. The enhanced result of the full cell demonstrates that the CPDs improve the RGO electrode affinity with Li+ and induce the formation of a uniform and robust SEI, suppressing the growth of Li dendrites during the repeated cycling. Impressively, the flexible pouch cell constructed with LFP cathode and Li@CPD@RGO-3 composite anode is able to power the LED at flat or bending states with desirable performance (Fig. 5e). Furthermore, the transparent pouch full cells exhibit the flexibility of the composite anode ( Fig. S17 ).

In summary, flexible CPD@GO substrates obtained by the one-step microwave method are developed as flexible Li metal host. Nanoscale CPDs with lithiophilic functional groups adjust the interlayer space of GO and act as nucleation sites to induce Li electrodeposition between layers and on the surface. As expected, the dendrite-free Li deposition is achieved. Rich surface functional groups modify the components of SEI with high ionic conductivity LiF and Li3N. CPD@RGO-3 substrates display stable CE of 96.0 % for 130th cycle at 1 mA cm−2 and decent cycling stability (voltage hysteresis is 38.1 mV initially, 10.5 mV after operation 2600 h) at 1.0 mA cm−2 with a capacity of 1.0 mAh cm−2. This work offers a feasible way to anchor lithiophilic nano-CDs on current collector to suppress the growth of Li dendrite and illustrates an insight for application of carbon materials in energy storage fields.

Guo-Duo Yang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Yan-Fei Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Lei Ding: Validation, Data curation. Zhuo Wang: Validation, Data curation. Su-Min Zhou: Validation, Data curation. Xing-Long Wu: Visualization. Jin Yang: Visualization, Supervision. Hai-Zhu Sun: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22035001 and 22275030), Jilin Provincial Education Department (JJKH20231304KJ) and Science and Technology Achievements Transformation Fund Project of Northeast Normal University (CGZH202203).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2024.159535.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

J. Am. Chem. Soc.

Adv. Mater.
